**Gravesend** [/ˌɡreɪvzˈɛnd/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") is a town in northwest [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), England, situated 21 miles (35 km) east-southeast of [Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross "Charing Cross") (central London) on the [south bank](/wiki/Bank_(geography) "Bank (geography)") of the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames"), opposite [Tilbury](/wiki/Tilbury "Tilbury") in [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"). Located in the [Diocese of Rochester](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Rochester "Anglican Diocese of Rochester"), it is the administrative centre of the borough of [Gravesham](/wiki/Gravesham "Gravesham"). Gravesend marks the eastern limit of the [Greater London Built-up Area](/wiki/Greater_London_Built-up_Area "Greater London Built-up Area"), as defined by the UK [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"). It had a population of roughly 60,250 in 2021.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Its geographical situation has given Gravesend strategic importance throughout the [maritime](/wiki/Maritime_history "Maritime history") and [communications history](/wiki/History_of_communication "History of communication") of [South East England](/wiki/South_East_England "South East England"). A [Thames Gateway](/wiki/Thames_Gateway "Thames Gateway") [commuter town](/wiki/Commuter_town "Commuter town"), it retains strong links with the River Thames, not least through the [Port of London Authority](/wiki/Port_of_London_Authority "Port of London Authority") Pilot Station, and has witnessed rejuvenation since the advent of [High Speed 1](/wiki/High_Speed_1 "High Speed 1") rail services via [Gravesend railway station](/wiki/Gravesend_railway_station "Gravesend railway station"). The station was recently refurbished[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] and has a new bridge.

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

Recorded as [Gravesham](/wiki/Gravesham "Gravesham") in the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") of 1086 when it belonged to [Odo, Earl of Kent](/wiki/Odo,_Earl_of_Kent "Odo, Earl of Kent") and [Bishop of Bayeux](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Bayeux "Roman Catholic Diocese of Bayeux"), the half-brother of [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror"), its name probably derives from *graaf-ham*: the home of the [reeve](/wiki/Sheriff "Sheriff") or bailiff of the [lord of the manor](/wiki/Lord_of_the_manor "Lord of the manor").

Another theory suggests that the name *Gravesham* may be a corruption of the words *grafs-ham* – a place "at the end of the grove".[[3]](#cite_note-3) Frank Carr[[4]](#cite_note-4) asserts that the name derives from the Saxon *Gerevesend*, the end of the authority of the [Portreeve](/wiki/Portreeve "Portreeve") (originally *Portgereve*, chief town administrator).

In the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), a place called ['s-Gravenzande](/wiki/%27s-Gravenzande "'s-Gravenzande") is found with its name translating into "Sand (or sandy area) belonging to the Count". The *'s* is a contraction of the old Dutch genitive article *des*, and translates into plain English as *of the*. In [Brooklyn, New York](/wiki/Brooklyn,_New_York "Brooklyn, New York"), the neighbourhood of [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn") is said by some to have been named for 's-Gravenzande,[[5]](#cite_note-5) though its founding by the English religious dissenter [Lady Deborah Moody](/wiki/Deborah_Moody "Deborah Moody") in 1645 suggests that it may be named after Gravesend, England. Lady Deborah was originally from London and is credited with being the first woman to found a settlement in the New World.

The Domesday spelling is its earliest known historical record;[[6]](#cite_note-6) all other spellings – in the later (c. 1100) Domesday *Monachorum* and in *Textus Roffensis* the town is *Gravesend* and *Gravesende*, respectively. The variation *Graveshend* can be seen in a court record of 1422, where [Edmund de Langeford](/wiki/Langford,_Bedfordshire "Langford, Bedfordshire") was [parson](/wiki/Parson "Parson"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) and attributed to where the graves ended after the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death"). The municipal title Gravesham was formally adopted in 1974 as the name for the new [borough](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality").[[8]](#cite_note-Hiscock-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age") implements have been found in the locality since the 1900s, as has evidence of an [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") settlement at nearby [Springhead](/wiki/Springhead,_Kent "Springhead, Kent"). Extensive [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") remains have been found at nearby [Vagniacae](/wiki/Springhead,_Kent "Springhead, Kent"), and Gravesend lies immediately to the north of the [Roman road](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road") connecting London with the Kent coast – now called [Watling Street](/wiki/Watling_Street "Watling Street"). [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") recorded [mills](/wiki/Watermill "Watermill"), [hythes](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/hythe "wikt:hythe"), and [fisheries](/wiki/Fishery "Fishery") here.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Milton_Chantry.jpg/250px-Milton_Chantry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milton_Chantry.jpg)

Milton Chantry, built c. 1320

[Milton Chantry](/wiki/Milton_Chantry "Milton Chantry")[[10]](#cite_note-10) is Gravesend's oldest surviving building and dates from the early 14th century. It was refounded as a [chapel](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel") in 1320/21 on the original site of a former [leper](/wiki/Leper "Leper") hospital founded in 1189. It is a [Grade II\*](/wiki/Grade_II* "Grade II*") listed building.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Gravesend has one of the oldest surviving [markets](/wiki/Marketplace "Marketplace") in the country. Its earliest charter dates from 1268, with town status being granted to the two parishes of [Gravesend](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church,_Gravesend "St George's Church, Gravesend") and [Milton](/wiki/Milton-next-Gravesend "Milton-next-Gravesend") by [King Henry III](/wiki/Henry_III_of_England "Henry III of England") in its Charter of Incorporation of that year. The first mayor of Gravesend was elected in 1268 but the first town hall was not built until 1573. The current [Gravesend Town Hall](/wiki/Gravesend_Town_Hall "Gravesend Town Hall") was completed in 1764: although it ceased to operate as a seat of government in 1968 when the new [Gravesham Civic Centre](/wiki/Gravesham_Civic_Centre "Gravesham Civic Centre") was opened, it remained in use as a [magistrates' court](/wiki/Magistrates%27_court "Magistrates' court") until 2000. It now operates as a venue for weddings and civil partnership ceremonies.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1380, during the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"), Gravesend [was raided](/wiki/Castilian_attack_on_Gravesend "Castilian attack on Gravesend") by a French [fleet](/wiki/Naval_fleet "Naval fleet").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1401, a further [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") was granted, allowing the men of the town to operate boats between [London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") and the town; these became known as the "Long Ferry". It became the preferred form of passage, because of the perils of road travel (see below).

On Gravesend's river front are the remains of a [device fort](/wiki/Device_fort "Device fort") built by command of [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") in 1543.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
In March 1617, [John Rolfe](/wiki/John_Rolfe "John Rolfe") and his Native American wife Rebecca ([Pocahontas](/wiki/Pocahontas "Pocahontas")), with their two-year-old son, [Thomas](/wiki/Thomas_Rolfe "Thomas Rolfe"), boarded a ship in London bound for [the Commonwealth of Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia");[[14]](#cite_note-14) the ship had only sailed as far as Gravesend before Rebecca fell ill,[[15]](#cite_note-15) and she died shortly after she was taken ashore. It is not known what caused her death.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Her funeral and interment took place on 21 March 1617 at the [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church") of [St George, Gravesend](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church,_Gravesend "St George's Church, Gravesend").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The site of her grave was underneath the church's [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel"), though since the previous church was destroyed by fire in 1727 her exact resting place is unknown.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Thomas Rolfe survived, but was placed under the supervision of [Sir Lewis Stukley](/wiki/Lewis_Stukley "Lewis Stukley") at [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth"), before being sent to his uncle, Henry Rolfe whilst John Rolfe and his late wife's assistant [Tomocomo](/wiki/Tomocomo "Tomocomo") reached America under the captaincy of [Sir Samuel Argall](/wiki/Samuel_Argall "Samuel Argall")'s ship. Pocahontas (real name: Matoaka) is an important figure in both American and British history.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/New_Tavern_Fort%2C_Gravesend_Kent_UK.JPG/250px-New_Tavern_Fort%2C_Gravesend_Kent_UK.JPG)](/wiki/File:New_Tavern_Fort,_Gravesend_Kent_UK.JPG)

Interior of New Tavern Fort

At Fort Gardens[[19]](#cite_note-19) is the New Tavern Fort,[[20]](#cite_note-20) built during the 1780s and extensively rebuilt by [Major-General Charles Gordon](/wiki/Charles_George_Gordon "Charles George Gordon") between 1865 and 1879; it is now the [Chantry Heritage Centre](/wiki/Milton_Chantry "Milton Chantry"), under the care of [Gravesend Local History Society](/wiki/English_local_history "English local history").[[21]](#cite_note-21) The fort is a [scheduled monument](/wiki/Scheduled_monument "Scheduled monument").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Journeys by road to Gravesend were historically quite hazardous, since the main [London-Dover road](/wiki/Transport_in_Kent "Transport in Kent") crossed [Blackheath](/wiki/Blackheath,_London "Blackheath, London"), notorious for its [highwaymen](/wiki/Highwaymen "Highwaymen"). [Stagecoaches](/wiki/Stagecoach "Stagecoach") from London to [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury "Canterbury"), [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover") and [Faversham](/wiki/Faversham "Faversham") used Gravesend as one of their "stages" as did those coming north from [Tonbridge](/wiki/Tonbridge "Tonbridge"). In 1840 there were 17 coaches picking up and setting down passengers and changing horses each way per day. There were two coaching inns on what is now Old Road East: *the Prince of Orange* and *the Lord Nelson*.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Post coaches](/wiki/Post_coach "Post coach") had been plying the route for at least two centuries: [Samuel Pepys](/wiki/Samuel_Pepys "Samuel Pepys") records having stopped off at Gravesend in 1650 en route to the Royal Dockyards at [Chatham](/wiki/Chatham,_Kent "Chatham, Kent").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

A permanent military presence was established in the town when [Milton Barracks](/wiki/Milton_Barracks "Milton Barracks") opened in 1862.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Although much of the town's economy continued to be connected with maritime trade, since the 19th century other major employers have been the cement and paper industries.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

From 1932 to 1956, an airport was located to the east of the town. On Sunday 5 February 1939, [Alex Henshaw](/wiki/Alex_Henshaw "Alex Henshaw") commenced his record-breaking flight to [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town") and back from here. He completed the flight in 39 hours 36 minutes over the next four days; his record still stands. Originally a civilian [airfield](/wiki/Airfield "Airfield"), during World War II it became a fighter station, [RAF Gravesend](/wiki/RAF_Gravesend "RAF Gravesend"), and so Gravesend was heavily bombed by the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe"). In 1956 the site was taken over by Gravesend Borough Council; a large housing estate, known as Riverview Park, was built on its site.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Governance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Gravesend_Town_Hall-geograph.org-3552497.jpg/250px-Gravesend_Town_Hall-geograph.org-3552497.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravesend_Town_Hall-geograph.org-3552497.jpg)

[Gravesend Town Hall](/wiki/Gravesend_Town_Hall "Gravesend Town Hall")

Gravesend is the principal town of the Borough of Gravesham.[[28]](#cite_note-28) The borough was formed on 1 April 1974, under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972"), by the merger of the [Municipal Borough of Gravesend](/wiki/Municipal_Borough_of_Gravesend "Municipal Borough of Gravesend") and [Northfleet Urban District Council](/wiki/Northfleet_Urban_District_Council "Northfleet Urban District Council") along with several parishes from Strood Rural District. Gravesend was incorporated as a Municipal Borough in 1835 under the [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835") and Northfleet was constituted an Urban District in 1894 under the [Local Government Act 1894](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1894 "Local Government Act 1894"): Gravesend absorbed Milton (1914), Denton, Chalk and part of [Northfleet](/wiki/Northfleet "Northfleet"), including Claphall, Singlewell and King's Farm (1935).

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Gravesendmap_1946.jpg/250px-Gravesendmap_1946.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravesendmap_1946.jpg)

A map of Gravesend, from 1946

Gravesend is located at a point where the higher land – the lowest point of the [dip slope](/wiki/Dip_slope "Dip slope") of the [North Downs](/wiki/North_Downs "North Downs") – reaches the Thames. To the east are the low-lying Shorne Marshes; to the west, beyond [Northfleet](/wiki/Northfleet "Northfleet") and the Swanscombe Marshes. The settlement was thus established as it was a good [landing place](/wiki/Port "Port"): it was also sheltered by the prominent height of what is now called [Windmill Hill](/wiki/Windmill_Hill,_Kent "Windmill Hill, Kent") (*see Landmarks below*); although Windmill Hill still remains a dominant feature, Gravesend's highest point is actually further inland at Marling Cross, adjacent to the [A2](/wiki/A2_road_(Great_Britain) "A2 road (Great Britain)").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

From its origins as a landing place and [shipping port](/wiki/Port "Port"), Gravesend gradually extended southwards and eastwards. Better-off people from London visited the town during the summer months; at first by boat, and then by railway. More extensive building began after World War I; this increased after World War II, when many of the housing estates in the locality were built.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Gravesend's built-up areas comprise *Painters Ash*, adjacent to the A2; *King's Farm* (most of King's Farm estate was built in the 1920s); and *Christianfields*. The latter housing estate was completely rebuilt in a 6-year project from 2007 to 2013. There is also the aforementioned Riverview Park estate built on the old RAF field in the south-east, in the 1960s, and Singlewell, which is adjacent to the [A2](/wiki/A2_road_(England) "A2 road (England)") in the South

Part of the southern built-up area of the town was originally two separate rural parishes: *viz*, [Cobham](/wiki/Cobham,_Kent "Cobham, Kent") and [Northfleet](/wiki/Northfleet "Northfleet").

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

Gravesend has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") similar to much of southern England, being accorded [Köppen Climate Classification-subtype](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_Climate_Classification "Köppen Climate Classification") of "[Cfb](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate")" (Marine West Coast Climate).[[31]](#cite_note-31)

On 10 August 2003, Gravesend recorded one of the highest temperatures since records began in the United Kingdom, with a reading of 38.1 °C (100.6 °F),[[32]](#cite_note-32) only beaten by [Brogdale](/wiki/Brogdale "Brogdale"), near [Faversham](/wiki/Faversham "Faversham"), 26 miles (42 km) to the ESE.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-MORecord-34) Gravesend, which has a [Met Office](/wiki/Met_Office "Met Office") site,[[35]](#cite_note-35) reports its data each hour.

Being inland and yet relatively close to [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"), Gravesend enjoys a somewhat more continental climate than the coastal areas of Kent, Essex and East Anglia and also compared to western parts of Britain. It is therefore less cloudy, drier, and less prone to Atlantic [depressions](/wiki/Low-pressure_area "Low-pressure area") with their associated wind and rain than western parts, as well as being hotter in summer and colder in winter.

Thus Gravesend continues to record higher temperatures in [summer](/wiki/Summer "Summer"), sometimes being the hottest place in the country, *e.g.* on the warmest day of 2011, when temperatures reached 33.1 °C.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Additionally, the town holds at least two records for the year 2010, of 30.9 °C[[37]](#cite_note-37) and 31.7 °C.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Another record was set during England's Indian summer of 2011 with 29.9 °C., the highest temperature ever recorded in the UK for October. In 2016 the warmest day of the year occurred very late on 13 September with a very high temperature of 34.4C

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Demography")]

Since 1990 the economy of [Gravesham](/wiki/Gravesham "Gravesham") has changed from one based on [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry") to being [service-based](/wiki/Service_industry "Service industry"). The borough's estimated population in 2012 was 101,700: a 6,000 increase in less than a decade. It has a high population density (almost 10 people per hectare) compared to nationally; it has a relatively young population (40% of the population are below 30); and 60% of the population are of working age.

Based upon figures from the 2021 census, the second largest religious group in the borough are [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") who at that time made up 8% of the population. However, if the term belief is used, [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") are most numerous at more than (49%), [non-religious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") (32.1%) and third [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhs "Sikhs") (8%).[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Shopping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Gravesend_High_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_931446.jpg/250px-Gravesend_High_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_931446.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravesend_High_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_931446.jpg)

High Street, Gravesend in 2008

Gravesend today is a commercial and commuter town, providing a local shopping district, including the St Georges shopping complex, the Thamesgate shopping centre and a regular [farmers' market](/wiki/Farmers%27_market "Farmers' market").[[40]](#cite_note-40) It also used to have a Debenhams and Wilko, but they closed down.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) Gravesend Market Hall, located in the heart of the town, was first chartered in 1268.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

### Gravesend Town Pier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Gravesend Town Pier")]

Main article: [Gravesend town pier](/wiki/Gravesend_town_pier "Gravesend town pier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Town_Pier_Gravesend.JPG/250px-Town_Pier_Gravesend.JPG)](/wiki/File:Town_Pier_Gravesend.JPG)

Town Pier Gravesend

Gravesend has the world's oldest surviving [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") [pier](/wiki/Pier "Pier"), built in 1834.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
It is a unique structure having the first known iron cylinders used in its construction. The pier was completely refurbished in 2004 and now features a bar and restaurant;[[45]](#cite_note-45) with public access to the pier head when the premises are open.[[46]](#cite_note-46)
A recent £2 million investment in a pontoon is now in place at the pier head onto the Thames, which provides for small and medium-sized craft to land at Gravesend. On 17 September 2012, the [Gravesend–Tilbury Ferry](/wiki/Gravesend%E2%80%93Tilbury_Ferry "Gravesend–Tilbury Ferry"), relocated to the Town Pier, from its previous terminal in nearby West Street, though the ferry service was discontinued in the 2020's due to funding issues

### Royal Terrace Pier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Royal Terrace Pier")]

Further information: [Royal Terrace Pier](/wiki/Royal_Terrace_Pier "Royal Terrace Pier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Another_view_of_Royal_Terrace_Pier_and_London_River_House_Gravesend.JPG/250px-Another_view_of_Royal_Terrace_Pier_and_London_River_House_Gravesend.JPG)](/wiki/File:Another_view_of_Royal_Terrace_Pier_and_London_River_House_Gravesend.JPG)

A view of Royal Terrace Pier and London River House Gravesend

Built in 1844, the initial construction was funded by the Gravesend Freehold Investment Company, at a cost of £9,200. It was where [Princess Alexandra of Denmark](/wiki/Alexandra_of_Denmark "Alexandra of Denmark") arrived on her way to marry [Edward, Prince of Wales (later King Edward VII)](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") in March 1865,[[47]](#cite_note-Listed-47) and [River pilots](/wiki/River_pilot "River pilot") have been based here since the late 19th century.

Today, Royal Terrace Pier is in constant 24-hour use, as part of the [Port of London Authority](/wiki/Port_of_London_Authority "Port of London Authority") main operations centre and [Gravesend RNLI Lifeboat Station](/wiki/Gravesend_Lifeboat_Station "Gravesend Lifeboat Station"), one of four lifeboat stations situated on the River Thames;[[48]](#cite_note-48) thus, its public access is available only occasionally during the year. It is T-shaped, with a [pontoon](/wiki/Pontoon_(boat) "Pontoon (boat)") at its pier head. Like the **Town Pier**, [Royal Terrace Pier](/wiki/Royal_Terrace_Pier "Royal Terrace Pier") is also a [Grade II](/wiki/Grade_II "Grade II") listed structure.[[47]](#cite_note-Listed-47)

### Gravesend Clock Tower, Milton Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Gravesend Clock Tower, Milton Road")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Clocktower_02.jpg/250px-Clocktower_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clocktower_02.jpg)

The Clock Tower located at the top of Harmer Street, Gravesend, Kent

Situated at the junction of Milton Road and Harmer Street, its foundation stone was laid on 6 September 1887. The memorial stone records that the [clock tower](/wiki/Clock_tower "Clock tower") was erected by public subscription (£700 was raised toward its construction) and dedicated to [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"), to commemorate the 50th year of her reign.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Built of Portland and Dumfries stone and backed by London stock brick, the design of the structure is based on the design of the [Elizabeth Tower](/wiki/Elizabeth_Tower "Elizabeth Tower") in the Palace of Westminster, which houses [Big Ben](/wiki/Clock_Tower,_Palace_of_Westminster "Clock Tower, Palace of Westminster"). The centre of the clock itself is measured at 50 feet (15 m) above ground and the face measures 5 ft 6 in (1.68 m) in diameter. The tower is Grade II [listed](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Pocahontas statue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Pocahontas statue")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Statue_of_Pocahontas_in_Gravesend.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Pocahontas_in_Gravesend.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Pocahontas_in_Gravesend.jpg)

Statue of Pocahontas

An American sculptor, [William Ordway Partridge](/wiki/William_Ordway_Partridge "William Ordway Partridge"), created a life-size statue of the 17th-century [Native American princess](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") [Pocahontas](/wiki/Pocahontas "Pocahontas"), which was unveiled at [Jamestown, Virginia](/wiki/Jamestown,_Virginia "Jamestown, Virginia") in 1922. [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_II "Queen Elizabeth II") viewed this statue in 1957 and again on 4 May 2007, while visiting Jamestown on the 400th anniversary of foundation, it being the first successful [English colonial settlement](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") in America.

On 5 October 1958, an exact replica of Partridge's statue was dedicated as a memorial to Pocahontas at [St George's Parish Church](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church,_Gravesend "St George's Church, Gravesend"). The [Governor of Virginia](/wiki/Governor_of_Virginia "Governor of Virginia") presented the statue as a gift to the British people in 1958, a gesture prompted by The Queen's visit to [the USA](/wiki/United_States_of_America "United States of America") in the previous year.

In 2017, US Ambassador to the United Kingdom [Matthew Barzun](/wiki/Matthew_Barzun "Matthew Barzun") visited the statue to mark the 400th anniversary of the death and burial of Pocahontas in Gravesend. The Ambassador laid a floral tribute of 21 roses at its base, each symbolising one year of Pocahontas' life.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Windmill Hill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Windmill Hill")]

Main article: [Windmill Hill, Kent](/wiki/Windmill_Hill,_Kent "Windmill Hill, Kent")

Windmill Hill, named after its former windmills, offers extensive views across the [Thames](/wiki/Thames "Thames") and was a popular spot for Victorian visitors to the town because of the [camera obscura](/wiki/Camera_obscura "Camera obscura") installed at the *Old Mill* and for its tea gardens and other amusements.

The hill was the site of a [beacon](/wiki/Beacon "Beacon") in 1377, which was instituted by [King Richard II](/wiki/Richard_II_of_England "Richard II of England"), and still in use 200 years later at the time of the [Spanish Armada](/wiki/Spanish_Armada "Spanish Armada"), although the hill was then known as "Rouge Hill". A modern beacon was erected and lit in 1988, the 400th anniversary of the Armada.

It was during the reign of [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") that the first [windmill](/wiki/Windmill "Windmill") was placed at the highest point in Gravesend, 179 ft (55 m) overlooking the high-water mark of the river. One mill burnt down in 1763 but was replaced the following year and that too demolished in 1894. The last surviving windmill is reported as having been destroyed by fire during [Mafeking Night](/wiki/Mafeking,_South_Africa "Mafeking, South Africa") celebrations in 1900.

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") an Imperial German Navy airship passed over Windmill Hill, dropping bombs on it; today there are three markers indicating where these bombs struck.

### Gravesend Power Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Gravesend Power Station")]

[Gravesend Power Station](/wiki/Gravesend_power_station "Gravesend power station") (TQ 6575 7413) was built by the Gravesend Corporation in 1902–03 to supply local demand for electricity. It was built on the south side of the basin at the west end of the [Thames and Medway canal](/wiki/Thames_and_Medway_Canal "Thames and Medway Canal").[[52]](#cite_note-:0-52) The buildings were demolished in 1995.[[52]](#cite_note-:0-52)

## Gravesend and the River Thames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Gravesend and the River Thames")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/GravesendThames3443.JPG/250px-GravesendThames3443.JPG)](/wiki/File:GravesendThames3443.JPG)

MV *Armia Krajowa*, a Panamax vessel, being docked by Gravesend [tugboats](/wiki/Tugboat "Tugboat") to discharge coal at [Tilbury](/wiki/Tilbury "Tilbury")

The Thames has long been an important feature in Gravesend life, and may well have been the deciding factor for the first settlement there. One of the town's first distinctions was in being given the sole right to transport passengers to and from London by water in the late 14th century.

In the 16th century, [Catherine of Aragon](/wiki/Catherine_of_Aragon "Catherine of Aragon"), the wife of [Prince Arthur](/wiki/Arthur,_Prince_of_Wales "Arthur, Prince of Wales") and later the first wife of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII"), was expected to arrive in England at Gravesend.

The "Tilt Boat" was a familiar sight as it sailed along the Thames, the passengers protected from the weather by a canvas tilt (awning). The first steamboat plied its trade between Gravesend and London in the early 19th century, bringing with it a steadily increasing number of visitors to the Terrace Pier Gardens, Windmill Hill, Springhead Gardens and [Rosherville Gardens](/wiki/Rosherville_Gardens "Rosherville Gardens"). Gravesend soon became one of the first English [resort towns](/wiki/Resort_town "Resort town") and thrived from an early tourist trade.

Gravesend "[watermen](/wiki/Waterman_(occupation) "Waterman (occupation)")" were often in a family trade; and the town is the headquarters of the [Port of London Authority](/wiki/Port_of_London_Authority "Port of London Authority") [Control Centre](/wiki/Port_of_London "Port of London") (formerly known as *Thames Navigation Service*), has its headquarters at Gravesend, providing [maritime pilots](/wiki/Maritime_pilot "Maritime pilot") who play an important role in navigation on the River Thames.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

A dinghy at an unmodernised Gravesend was the backdrop to the 1952 thriller *[The Long Memory](/wiki/The_Long_Memory "The Long Memory")* starring [Sir John Mills](/wiki/Sir_John_Mills "Sir John Mills"). In the film, Mills plays a character living in poverty on a derelict fishing boat stranded in the mud flats.

Gravesend has one of England's oldest regattas retained from its strong maritime links with the Thames. Although the origins of the regatta are unknown it dates back at least to [Tudor times](/wiki/Tudor_era "Tudor era"). The races are traditionally competed by Gravesend [skiffs](/wiki/Skiffs "Skiffs"), 21-foot-long (6.4 m) [oaken](/wiki/Oak#Uses "Oak") round-bottomed, [clinker-built](/wiki/Clinker-built "Clinker-built") boats.

The *Thames Navigation Service* was first thought up between 1950 and 1952 by Cdr [Peter de Neumann](/wiki/Peter_de_Neumann "Peter de Neumann"), while he was captain of [HM Customs and Excise](/wiki/HM_Customs_and_Excise "HM Customs and Excise") cutter *Vigilant* based at **Gravesend Reach**. This idea followed from considering such incidents as the accidental ramming of HMS *Truculent* by the *Divina* in 1950, the collision with the *Nore Forts* by *Baalbek*, and the disastrous flooding of [Canvey](/wiki/Canvey "Canvey"), [Foulness](/wiki/Foulness "Foulness") and the East Coast in 1953. In these and other situations, rescue and intelligence gathering were severely hampered by a lack of centralised command and control, and lack of a detailed "picture". De Neumann resigned his command after returning *Vigilant* from the Spithead Review and joined the PLA, immediately suggesting, in a report to them submitted in 1953, that a feasibility study of such a system be carried out. He then oversaw its development and ultimate installation at Gravesend.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/St_Andrew%27s_Art_Centre_%26_Gallery_at_Bawley_Bay%2C_Gravesend.JPG/250px-St_Andrew%27s_Art_Centre_%26_Gallery_at_Bawley_Bay%2C_Gravesend.JPG)](/wiki/File:St_Andrew%27s_Art_Centre_%26_Gallery_at_Bawley_Bay,_Gravesend.JPG)

St Andrew's Art Centre & Gallery sits between Bawley Bay and Anchor Cove, both being the embarkation points for British colonists sailing to New Zealand and Australia in the early 19th century.

Until the building of [Tilbury Docks](/wiki/Port_of_Tilbury "Port of Tilbury"), on the opposite side of the river, between 1882 and 1886, Gravesend was the Thames's first port of entry. Thousands of emigrants, as well as large numbers of troops, embarked from here. Tilbury Docks have expanded considerably since, with the closure of all the [London Docks](/wiki/London_Docks "London Docks"). The entrance to the Docks is somewhat awkward, situated as it is on the sharp bend of the river, and boats often need [tugboat](/wiki/Tugboat "Tugboat") assistance, as do the larger ships moored at Tilbury landing stages. There have been many tug companies based at Gravesend: among them the Sun Company, the Alexandra Towing Company and, today, the Smith Howard Towing Company. [East Indiamen](/wiki/East_Indiamen "East Indiamen") traditionally stopped here at a point known as **Long Reach** to lighten their loads before sailing up the [Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") to moorings at [Blackwall](/wiki/Blackwall,_London "Blackwall, London").[[54]](#cite_note-54)

For some years after, war steamer excursions were run on the [MV *Royal Daffodil*](/wiki/MV_Royal_Daffodil_(1939) "MV Royal Daffodil (1939)") down the Thames from Gravesend to [France](/wiki/France "France"), but they ceased in 1966. Cruises are now operated by the [Lower Thames and Medway Passenger Boat Company](/wiki/Lower_Thames_and_Medway_Passenger_Boat_Company "Lower Thames and Medway Passenger Boat Company") up the river to [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich "Greenwich"). The cross-river passenger ferry to [Tilbury](/wiki/Tilbury "Tilbury") provides a long-established route to and from Essex. Before the [Dartford Crossing](/wiki/Dartford_Crossing "Dartford Crossing") came into being, there was a vehicle ferry at Gravesend as well.

There is a [RNLI lifeboat station](/wiki/Gravesend_Lifeboat_Station "Gravesend Lifeboat Station"), based at **Royal Terrace Pier**, which is one of the busiest in the country.[[55]](#cite_note-RNLINews-55)

### Thames and Medway Canal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Thames and Medway Canal")]

Main article: [Thames and Medway Canal](/wiki/Thames_and_Medway_Canal "Thames and Medway Canal")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/GravesendCanalBasin3456.JPG/250px-GravesendCanalBasin3456.JPG)](/wiki/File:GravesendCanalBasin3456.JPG)

Gravesend Canal Basin

The [Thames and Medway Canal](/wiki/Thames_and_Medway_Canal "Thames and Medway Canal") was opened for barge traffic in 1824. It ran from Gravesend on the Thames to [Frindsbury](/wiki/Frindsbury "Frindsbury") near [Strood](/wiki/Strood "Strood") on the [Medway](/wiki/Medway "Medway"). Although seven miles long, it had only two locks, each 94 ft (29 m) by 22 ft (6.7 m) in size, one at each end. Its most notable feature was the tunnel near [Strood](/wiki/Strood "Strood"), which was 3,946 yd (3,608 m) long, the second longest canal tunnel ever built in the UK. The great cost of the tunnel meant that the canal was not a commercial success.

After only 20 years, most of the canal was closed and the canal's tunnel was converted to railway use. Initially, canal and railway shared the tunnel, with the single track built on timber supports, but by 1847, canal use was abandoned and a double track laid. Today the Gravesend Canal Basin is used for the mooring of [pleasure craft](/wiki/Pleasure_craft "Pleasure craft"). Gravesend Sailing Club, which was founded so that working men could participate in the sport while still having to earn a living is based here. The lock has been dredged, and restoration and strengthening work has been carried out on the basin walls as part of the regeneration of the area.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Roads")]

The main roads through the town are the west–east [A226 road](/wiki/A226_road "A226 road") from [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford "Dartford") and beyond to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_Kent "Rochester, Kent"); and the [A227 road](/wiki/A227_road "A227 road") to [Tonbridge](/wiki/Tonbridge "Tonbridge"). The [A2 road](/wiki/A2_road_(Great_Britain) "A2 road (Great Britain)") passes two miles (3 km) south of Gravesend town centre; a mile stretch of it was rerouted in the early 2000s to take the traffic away from the south end of the town.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

On 26 March 2006 the first of the area's new [Fastrack](/wiki/Fastrack_(bus) "Fastrack (bus)") bus services, which use a combination of ordinary roads and dedicated 'bus tracks', opened. The service links to [Ebbsfleet International railway station](/wiki/Ebbsfleet_International_railway_station "Ebbsfleet International railway station"), [Greenhithe](/wiki/Greenhithe,_Kent "Greenhithe, Kent"), [Bluewater Shopping Centre](/wiki/Bluewater_(shopping_centre) "Bluewater (shopping centre)") and Dartford.

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Rail")]

Main articles: [Gravesend railway station](/wiki/Gravesend_railway_station "Gravesend railway station") and [Gravesend West Line](/wiki/Gravesend_West_Line "Gravesend West Line")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Gravesend_Railway_Station_2.jpg/250px-Gravesend_Railway_Station_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravesend_Railway_Station_2.jpg)

Gravesend's Victorian railway station modernised for HS1 in 2014

The historic [Gravesend Railway Station](/wiki/Gravesend_railway_station "Gravesend railway station"), opened in 1849, lies on the [North Kent Line](/wiki/North_Kent_Line "North Kent Line"). The [Gravesend West Line](/wiki/Gravesend_West_Line "Gravesend West Line"), terminating by the river and for some time operating as a [continental](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel") ferry connection, closed in 1968.

Gravesend is the primary [north Kent](/wiki/North_West_Kent "North West Kent") interchange for high speed and metro rail services. In December 2009, the full high-speed timetable between London and Kent came into force and passenger usage from Gravesend has exceeded expectations. High-speed services from [London St Pancras](/wiki/St_Pancras_railway_station "St Pancras railway station") and [Stratford International](/wiki/Stratford_International_railway_station "Stratford International railway station"), are offered via Gravesend to the [Medway](/wiki/Medway "Medway") towns, [Sittingbourne](/wiki/Sittingbourne "Sittingbourne"), [Faversham](/wiki/Faversham "Faversham"), [Margate](/wiki/Margate "Margate"), [Broadstairs](/wiki/Broadstairs "Broadstairs") and [Ramsgate](/wiki/Ramsgate "Ramsgate"). Some of these services continue to [Ashford International](/wiki/Ashford_International_railway_station "Ashford International railway station") via [Sandwich](/wiki/Sandwich_railway_station "Sandwich railway station") and [Dover Priory](/wiki/Dover_Priory_railway_station "Dover Priory railway station").

There are also metro services to [London Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross_railway_station "Charing Cross railway station"), [London Waterloo East](/wiki/Waterloo_East_railway_station "Waterloo East railway station") and [London Bridge](/wiki/London_Bridge_railway_station "London Bridge railway station") via [Sidcup](/wiki/Sidcup_railway_station "Sidcup railway station"), via [Woolwich Arsenal](/wiki/Woolwich_Arsenal_railway_station "Woolwich Arsenal railway station") and [Lewisham](/wiki/Lewisham_railway_station "Lewisham railway station") and [Bexleyheath](/wiki/Bexleyheath_railway_station "Bexleyheath railway station"), and to [Gillingham](/wiki/Gillingham_(Kent)_railway_station "Gillingham (Kent) railway station").

Unusually Gravesend features a Platform 0, one of the few in the country, it is used for terminating services from [London Cannon Street](/wiki/Cannon_Street_railway_station "Cannon Street railway station") or [London Charing Cross](/wiki/Charing_Cross_railway_station "Charing Cross railway station") via [Sidcup](/wiki/Sidcup "Sidcup").

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Buses")]

Gravesend is served by several [Arriva Kent Thameside](/wiki/Arriva_Kent_Thameside "Arriva Kent Thameside") bus services connecting the town with other areas in [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent") including [Dartford](/wiki/Dartford "Dartford"), [Bluewater](/wiki/Bluewater_(shopping_centre) "Bluewater (shopping centre)") and [Sevenoaks](/wiki/Sevenoaks "Sevenoaks") and to the [Medway Towns](/wiki/Medway_Towns "Medway Towns").  
Gravesend is also served by [Fastrack](/wiki/Fastrack_(bus) "Fastrack (bus)") bus services connecting the town with [Bluewater](/wiki/Bluewater_Shopping_Centre "Bluewater Shopping Centre"), [Darent Valley Hospital](/wiki/Darent_Valley_Hospital "Darent Valley Hospital") and Dartford.

### Ferry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ferry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/GravesendThames3401.JPG/250px-GravesendThames3401.JPG)](/wiki/File:GravesendThames3401.JPG)

The ferry from Tilbury coming alongside at Gravesend

Main article: [Gravesend–Tilbury Ferry](/wiki/Gravesend%E2%80%93Tilbury_Ferry "Gravesend–Tilbury Ferry")

Passenger ferry services to [Tilbury](/wiki/Tilbury "Tilbury"), [Essex](/wiki/Essex "Essex"), operated daily (except Sundays), from [Gravesend Town Pier](/wiki/Gravesend_town_pier "Gravesend town pier") until 31 March 2024.

### Footpaths

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Footpaths")]

The [Saxon Shore Way](/wiki/Saxon_Shore_Way "Saxon Shore Way"), a [long distance footpath](/wiki/Long_distance_footpath "Long distance footpath"), starts at Gravesend and traces the coast as in [Roman times](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") as far as [Hastings](/wiki/Hastings "Hastings"), [East Sussex](/wiki/East_Sussex "East Sussex"); 163 miles (262 km) in total. The [Wealdway](/wiki/Wealdway "Wealdway") also starts at the Town Pier, and continues almost due south over the [Weald](/wiki/Weald "Weald") to [Eastbourne](/wiki/Eastbourne "Eastbourne") in East Sussex where it links with [South Downs Way](/wiki/South_Downs_Way "South Downs Way"), a distance of 80 miles (128 km).

## Religious Buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Religious Buildings")]

The town's principal Anglican place of worship is the Church of St George, Gravesend. This [Georgian building](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") is a tourist attraction because of its association with Princess Pocahontas, as well as being the [parish church](/wiki/Parish_church "Parish church"). Gravesend has three other [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") parishes and [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), [United Reformed](/wiki/United_Reformed "United Reformed") and [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") churches as well as other smaller chapels.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Gravesend_Sikh_Temple_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1943692.jpg/250px-Gravesend_Sikh_Temple_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1943692.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravesend_Sikh_Temple_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1943692.jpg)

Guru Nanak Darbar Gurdwara, Gravesend

Gravesend has a significant [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") population of more than 15,000, representing over 15% of the town's population.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Its first [gurdwara](/wiki/Gurdwara "Gurdwara") was founded in 1956 by [Bhat Sikh](/wiki/Bhat_Sikhs "Bhat Sikhs") [Santokh Singh Takk](/wiki/Suraj_Parkash "Suraj Parkash") in Edwin Street with a second one opening, ten years later, in a former [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") church, but this gurdwara closed in 2010. The same year, one of Europe's largest [Sikh temples](/wiki/Sikh_temple "Sikh temple") was opened at a cost of £12 million.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Education")]

Gravesend many schools include [Northfleet School for Girls](/wiki/Northfleet_School_for_Girls "Northfleet School for Girls"); [Northfleet Technology College](/wiki/Northfleet_Technology_College "Northfleet Technology College") (Northfleet School for Boys, on the former sites of Northfleet Secondary School for Boys and Gravesend Technical High School for Boys); [Mayfield Grammar School](/wiki/Mayfield_Grammar_School "Mayfield Grammar School") (formerly [Gravesend Grammar School for Girls](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School_for_Girls "Gravesend Grammar School for Girls")); [St John's Catholic Comprehensive School](/wiki/St_John%27s_Catholic_Comprehensive_School "St John's Catholic Comprehensive School"); [Thamesview School](/wiki/Thamesview_School "Thamesview School") and [St George's Church of England School](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church_of_England_School "St George's Church of England School"). Gravesend also hosts [Gravesend Grammar School](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School"), a, consistently high-achieving grammar school and sixth form where 50% of students gained a Grade 7 (Grade A equivalent) or above in 2025.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are also primary age schools such as Whitehill Primary School, special schools and several independent schools, such as Bronte School and St Joseph's.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Finally, the town is also host to an Adult Education Centre.

## Health & Hospice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Health & Hospice")]

Gravesend Hospital was opened in 1854, following the donation of a site by the [6th Earl of Darnley](/wiki/Earl_of_Darnley "Earl of Darnley") in 1853; it had its origin on 2 December 1850, as a dispensary on the Milton Road "to assist the really destitute poor of Gravesend and Milton and vicinities ... unable to pay for medical aid". By 1893, 4,699 such people had benefited by its presence.

In 2004 the original building, and parts of the newer buildings were demolished to make way for a new community hospital. Gravesend Community Hospital provides a Minor Injury Unit, Dental services, Speech and Language therapy and Physiotherapy. It also has a Stroke Ward and offers inpatient care. The outpatient department provides care for much of the local area and is separate from those offered at Darent Valley Hospital. In addition, Gravesend emergency doctors out of hours service as well as podiatry are offered.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

In the town centre is a medium-sized medical clinic at Swan Yard, next to the Market car park. The medical clinic is only suitable for minor injuries, for major injuries and more severe or specialist appointments, a trip to [Darrent Valley Hospital](/wiki/Darent_Valley_Hospital "Darent Valley Hospital") is required. Several other doctors' surgeries are located in the area.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sport")]

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Football")]

The [Stonebridge Road](/wiki/Stonebridge_Road "Stonebridge Road") [football ground](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") at neighbouring [Northfleet](/wiki/Northfleet "Northfleet") is home to [Ebbsfleet United F.C.](/wiki/Ebbsfleet_United_F.C. "Ebbsfleet United F.C."), which changed its name from Gravesend and Northfleet F.C. in June 2007. Ebbsfleet currently plays in the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(English_football) "National League (English football)"), and the club [won the FA Trophy in May 2008](/wiki/FA_Trophy_2007-08 "FA Trophy 2007-08"). An agreement was reached for the [MyFootballClub](/wiki/MyFootballClub "MyFootballClub") online community to purchase a 75% stake in the club in November 2007, and its takeover was completed early in 2008.[[61]](#cite_note-Fleet_Takeover_Vote-61)

### Cricket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cricket")]

[Gravesend Cricket Club](/wiki/Gravesend_Cricket_Club "Gravesend Cricket Club") (founded in 1881 when the Harkaway and Clarence Cricket Clubs amalgamated) is based at the [Bat and Ball Ground](/wiki/Bat_and_Ball_Ground "Bat and Ball Ground") on Wrotham Road, where [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") has been played since its foundation at the behest of the [6th Earl of Darnley](/wiki/Earl_of_Darnley "Earl of Darnley") (grandfather of the celebrated England cricketer, [The Hon. Ivo Bligh](/wiki/Ivo_Bligh "Ivo Bligh"), later 8th Earl of Darnley) in the mid 19th century.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Rugby Union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Rugby Union")]

Gravesend has two [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") teams, [Gravesend Rugby Football Club](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend_Rugby_Football_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gravesend Rugby Football Club (page does not exist)") and Old Gravesendians RFC, which are situated next to each other opposite the [Gravesend Grammar School](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School").

Old Gravesendians RFC (founded in 1929)[[63]](#cite_note-63) consisted traditionally of former Gravesend Grammar School pupils. Prior to the forming of Old Gravesendians RFC, on leaving the Grammar School, former pupils had continued to engage in various sports through the Old Blues Association (founded in 1914).[[64]](#cite_note-64) Owing to World War I the Old Blues Association practically went to pieces with only one annual dinner having been held in 1914. After the war a reunion dinner was held in 1920, the second annual dinner, which restarted the Old Blues Association activities. The Old Gravesendians RFC was often referred to as 'Gravesend Old Blues' in match reports.

Old Gravesendians RFC continued to foster rugby in Gravesend during World War II by turning out a side every season. Since 2000 Old Gravesendians (Old G's) have reached six Kent Plate finals, winning two. They achieved promotion to London League rugby in 2009, but were relegated in 2009–10. Old G's put out three sides with the first team playing in [Shepherd Neame Kent 1](/wiki/Shepherd_Neame_Kent_1 "Shepherd Neame Kent 1") during the 2018–19 season. The team colours are light blue and dark blue.

### Rowing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Rowing")]

Rowing races have been held on the [River Thames](/wiki/River_Thames "River Thames") at Gravesend since at least 1698, with the first organised [Regatta](/wiki/Regatta "Regatta") recorded in 1715. The first Borough Regatta began in 1882,[[65]](#cite_note-65) setting the pattern for an annual event on the River Thames that is carried on to this day. The River Thames in Gravesend is home to the Gravesend Rowing Club (founded in 1878),[[66]](#cite_note-66) which the club claims is the oldest existing sporting club in Gravesend,[[67]](#cite_note-67) the Regatta Committee's skiff rowers, and Gravesend Sailing Club. [Gravesend Grammar School](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School") also hosts its own Rowing Club.

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Cycling")]

To the south of Gravesend on the ancient site of Watling Street on 43ha of land adjacent to the A2, [Cyclopark](/wiki/Cyclopark "Cyclopark"), a venue for [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") events and other activities has been developed.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The site which features [mountain bike](/wiki/Mountain_bike "Mountain bike") trails, a [road circuit](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing"), a [BMX](/wiki/BMX "BMX") racetrack and family cycling paths was formally opened in early 2012.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The Gravesend Historical Society meets regularly and produces a biannual magazine on its activities.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

[Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") lived at [Gad's Hill Place](/wiki/Gad%27s_Hill_Place "Gad's Hill Place"), 2 miles (3.2 km) east of Gravesend and specifically mentions the town and its environs in at least three of his novels. In *[David Copperfield](/wiki/David_Copperfield_(novel) "David Copperfield (novel)")* Mr. Peggotty, Ham and the Micawbers say their goodbyes and sail away from Gravesend to begin a new life in Australia. In *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations "Great Expectations")*, Pip, with accomplices, rows Magwitch from London downriver in expectation of waylaying a regular steamer (whilst under way in the Lower Hope, off Gravesend) bound for Hamburg. At St James' Church, in Cooling, Pip finds Magwitch hidden among the graves. Gravesend also appears in [The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers") by [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens"). potentially due to him living at a nearby residence near Gad's Hill Private School.

Gravesend is also briefly mentioned in the 1818 novel *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* by [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley") during Victor's travels through the United Kingdom with Clerval; ultimately culminating in Victor's residence in the [Orkney Islands](/wiki/Orkney_Islands "Orkney Islands").

[Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle") often mentioned Gravesend in his [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes") stories.

In the 1902 novel *[Heart of Darkness](/wiki/Heart_of_Darkness "Heart of Darkness")* by [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"), Charles Marlow's ship, anchored off Gravesend, is the setting where he tells his tale.

The 1952 film "[The Long Memory](/wiki/The_Long_Memory "The Long Memory")" starring [John Mills](/wiki/John_Mills "John Mills") was filmed in and around Gravesend. It features many squalid streets running down towards the river that even then were being progressively cleared for redevelopment. It is also possible to hear in the background steam engines working out of the now closed [Gravesend West Line](/wiki/Gravesend_West_Line "Gravesend West Line") West Street terminus.

*[The War Game](/wiki/The_War_Game "The War Game")* was a 1965 BBC television drama-documentary film depicting a nuclear war that was initially banned, and not broadcast until July 1985. The film was shot in Gravesend and in the other Kent towns of Tonbridge, Chatham and Dover, with a cast which was almost entirely made up of non-actors.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Statue of General Gordon at Gravesend

* [Sir Edwin Arnold](/wiki/Edwin_Arnold "Edwin Arnold") (1832–1904), English poet and journalist whose most prominent work as a poet was *The Light of Asia* (1879).[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* [Gemma Arterton](/wiki/Gemma_Arterton "Gemma Arterton") (born 1986), actress, was born at Northfleet and attended [Gravesend Grammar School for Girls](/wiki/Mayfield_Grammar_School "Mayfield Grammar School").
* [Sir Derek Barton](/wiki/Derek_Barton "Derek Barton") (1918–1998), English chemist and [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") winner for "contributions to the development of the concept of conformation and its application in chemistry".
* [Admiral Sir Francis Beaufort](/wiki/Francis_Beaufort "Francis Beaufort") (1774–1857), creator of the Beaufort Scale, was stationed at Gravesend.
* [Sir Peter Blake](/wiki/Peter_Blake_(artist) "Peter Blake (artist)") (born 1932), artist who trained at Gravesend School of Art. *The Blake Gallery* has recently been opened at the Woodville Halls in the town.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
* [George Box](/wiki/George_Box "George Box") (1919–2013), renowned statistician, and a recipient of the [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society").
* [Laura Coombs](/wiki/Laura_Coombs "Laura Coombs") (born 1991), footballer for [England](/wiki/England_women%27s_national_football_team "England women's national football team").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") is associated with Gravesend and villages around the borough. Many of the links between him and Gravesham are still in evidence – Gravesend he visited, at Chalk he spent his honeymoon, at Higham he lived and died, and at Cobham he found inspiration for *[The Pickwick Papers](/wiki/The_Pickwick_Papers "The Pickwick Papers")*.
* [Jessica Dismorr](/wiki/Jessica_Dismorr "Jessica Dismorr") (1885-1939), a member of the [Vorticism](/wiki/Vorticism "Vorticism") art movement, was born in Gravesend.
* [Carl Daniel Ekman](/wiki/Carl_Daniel_Ekman "Carl Daniel Ekman") (1845–1904) Swedish chemist and paper-maker who relocated to Gravesend.[[74]](#cite_note-74)
* [Major-General](/wiki/Major-General "Major-General") [Charles Gordon](/wiki/Charles_George_Gordon "Charles George Gordon") (1833–1885), lived in the town from 1865 to 1871. As commander of the Royal Engineers, he supervised the construction of the forts guarding the Thames downstream from Gravesend, at [New Tavern Fort](/wiki/New_Tavern_Fort "New Tavern Fort") in the town, [Shornemead Fort](/wiki/Shornemead_Fort "Shornemead Fort") on the Thames's south bank, and [Coalhouse Fort](/wiki/Coalhouse_Fort "Coalhouse Fort") on the north in Essex. While in Gravesend, Gordon devoted himself to the welfare of the town's "poor boys", establishing a [Sunday School](/wiki/Sunday_School "Sunday School") and providing food and clothes for them from his Army wages. His links with Gravesend are commemorated locally on the embankment at the Riverside Leisure Area, which is known as the Gordon Promenade, and at Khartoum Place that lies just to the south.[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* [Paul Greengrass](/wiki/Paul_Greengrass "Paul Greengrass") (born 1955) film director was educated at [Gravesend Grammar School for Boys](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School").
* [Marc Guehi](/wiki/Marc_Guehi "Marc Guehi") (born 2000), English professional footballer for [Crystal Palace F.C.](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C."), was educated at [St George's Church of England School](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church_of_England_School "St George's Church of England School"), in Gravesend.[[76]](#cite_note-76)
* [Thom Gunn](/wiki/Thom_Gunn "Thom Gunn") (1929–2004), Anglo-American poet, was born in Gravesend. His most famous collection, *The Man With Night Sweats* (1992), is dominated by AIDS-related elegies.[[77]](#cite_note-donyt-77) He relocated to [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [California](/wiki/California "California") in 1954 to teach writing at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") and remain close to Mike his partner whom he met whilst at university.
* [Katharine Hamnett](/wiki/Katharine_Hamnett "Katharine Hamnett") (born 1947), fashion designer.
* [William Hanneford-Smith](/wiki/William_Hanneford-Smith "William Hanneford-Smith") (1878–1954) publisher
* [Adam Holloway](/wiki/Adam_Holloway "Adam Holloway") (born 1965), former [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP) 2005-2024, lives on Darnley Road in the town.
* [Frederick Holbourn](/wiki/Frederick_Holbourn "Frederick Holbourn") (1896-1967), war pensioner activist
* [Mary Lucas Keene](/wiki/Mary_Lucas_Keene "Mary Lucas Keene") (1885–1977), professor of anatomy[[78]](#cite_note-78)
* [Shadrach Jones](/wiki/Shadrach_Jones "Shadrach Jones") (c.1822–1895) New Zealand doctor, auctioneer, hotel-owner and impresario; born in Gravesend.
* [Joshua Laqeretabua](/wiki/Joshua_Laqeretabua "Joshua Laqeretabua") (born 2005), Fijian professional footballer for [Charlton Athletic F.C.](/wiki/Charlton_Athletic_F.C. "Charlton Athletic F.C.") and [Fiji national under-20 football team](/wiki/Fiji_national_under-20_football_team "Fiji national under-20 football team"), was educated at [St George's Church of England School](/wiki/St_George%27s_Church_of_England_School "St George's Church of England School"), in Gravesend.
* [John MacGregor](/wiki/John_MacGregor_(sportsman) "John MacGregor (sportsman)") (1825–1892), English writer, who designed the "Rob Roy" canoe.[[79]](#cite_note-79)
* [Mitch Pinnock](/wiki/Mitch_Pinnock "Mitch Pinnock") (born 1994), English professional footballer, was born in the town. He currently plays for [Northampton Town](/wiki/Northampton_Town_F.C. "Northampton Town F.C.").
* [Pocahontas](/wiki/Pocahontas "Pocahontas") (1595–1617), the first Native American girl or woman to visit England. She was taken ill on her return voyage to America, and died aged 21 after coming ashore at Gravesend. She was buried under the chancel of St George's parish church.
* [Harry Reid](/wiki/Harry_Reid_(actor) "Harry Reid (actor)") (born 1992), actor who appeared in [EastEnders](/wiki/EastEnders "EastEnders") as Ben Mitchell, was born and lives in Gravesend. He attended [Northfleet Technology College](/wiki/Northfleet_Technology_College "Northfleet Technology College") (formerly known as Northfleet School for Boys).[[80]](#cite_note-80) Trained in [acting](/wiki/Acting "Acting"), physical theatre and musical theatre at Miskin Theatre in [Dartford, Kent](/wiki/Dartford "Dartford").[[81]](#cite_note-81)
* The composer [Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov](/wiki/Nikolai_Rimsky-Korsakov "Nikolai Rimsky-Korsakov") (1844–1908) was an officer in the Russian Navy and was posted to Gravesend in 1862, where he wrote part of his first symphony, said to be the first such style of composition attempted by a [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") composer.
* [Paul Ritter](/wiki/Paul_Ritter "Paul Ritter") (1966-2021), actor best known for [Friday Night Dinner](/wiki/Friday_Night_Dinner "Friday Night Dinner"), [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl "Chernobyl") and [Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Half-Blood_Prince_(film) "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince (film)")
* [David Rutley](/wiki/David_Rutley "David Rutley") (born 1961 at Gravesend), first [Mormon](/wiki/Mormon "Mormon") UK [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MP).
* [Chris Simmons](/wiki/Chris_Simmons "Chris Simmons") (born 1975 at Gravesend), television and stage actor best known for his roles as DC [Mickey Webb](/wiki/Mickey_Webb "Mickey Webb") in *[The Bill](/wiki/The_Bill "The Bill")*, Mark Garland in *[EastEnders](/wiki/EastEnders "EastEnders")* and Stuart Summer in *[Hollyoaks](/wiki/Hollyoaks "Hollyoaks")*.
* [Charles Stewart, 3rd Duke of Richmond](/wiki/Charles_Stewart,_3rd_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Stewart, 3rd Duke of Richmond"), resided at [Cobham Hall](/wiki/Cobham_Hall "Cobham Hall"), 5 miles (8 km) south east of Gravesend, until 1672 (followed by his descendants, the [Earls of Darnley](/wiki/Earl_of_Darnley "Earl of Darnley")).
* [Olamide Sodeke](/wiki/Olamide_Sodeke "Olamide Sodeke") (born 1997) English professional rugby player for [Saracens](/wiki/Saracens "Saracens"), was educated at [Gravesend Grammar School](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School")
* [Fikayo Tomori](/wiki/Fikayo_Tomori "Fikayo Tomori") (born 1997) English professional footballer for [AC Milan](/wiki/AC_Milan "AC Milan"), was educated at [Gravesend Grammar School](/wiki/Gravesend_Grammar_School "Gravesend Grammar School")

## Twin towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gravesend&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Twin towns")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of twin towns and sister cities in the United Kingdom")

Gravesend is [twinned](/wiki/Twin_towns_and_sister_cities "Twin towns and sister cities") with:

* [Cambrai](/wiki/Cambrai "Cambrai"), France[[82]](#cite_note-Archant_twinning_3-82)
* [Chesterfield, Virginia](/wiki/Chesterfield,_Virginia "Chesterfield, Virginia"), United States
* [Neumünster](/wiki/Neum%C3%BCnster "Neumünster"), Germany
* [Brunswick, Victoria](/wiki/Brunswick,_Victoria "Brunswick, Victoria"), Australia
